steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The subject of the main clause is "President Mikhail Gorbachev's reforms." The long phrase "by helping the Tuvan Republic...assert their prerogatives" is a prepositional modifier — it explains how the reforms acted, but it is not the main verb. The blank must be the finite main verb that completes the independent clause, telling us what the reforms did.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'contributed': a finite past-tense verb. Plugging it in gives "reforms...contributed to the Soviet Union's breakup" — a complete independent clause with a true subject-verb pair. ✓
  • Choice B — 'contributing': a present participle. It cannot serve as a main verb on its own (it would need an auxiliary like "were"). Using it leaves the sentence with no finite verb — a fragment. ✗
  • Choice C — 'having contributed': a perfect participle. Like "contributing," it is non-finite and cannot anchor an independent clause without an auxiliary. The result is still a fragment. ✗
  • Choice D — 'which contributed': turns the clause into a relative clause modifying "reforms." The sentence then has a subject ("reforms") followed by a relative clause but no main verb — a fragment. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
